text: The surgeon inserts a varus needle into the left upper quadrant at Palmer's point, initiating the procedure.
The surgeon positions the camera to view just to the left of the umbilicus, checks the placement of the varus needle, and then inserts the next port to the right of the umbilicus along the midline.
The surgeon places a right lateral port for the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon places the final port in the left upper quadrant, completing the setup for the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon completes the port placement, resulting in a hockey stick configuration of the ports on the abdominal surface.
With the surgical ports positioned in the left upper quadrant, right lateral, and near the umbilicus, the robotic system is docked to the ports for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon docks the robotic system and places the camera in port number two, inserts the bipolar instrument in port number one, and positions the hook instrument in port number three.
The surgeon places a ProGrasp instrument into arm four of the robotic system, completing the setup of the surgical instruments.
The robotic arm four is used to retract the gallbladder cephalad, elevating it to improve exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses robotic arms one and three, equipped with a bipolar and hook instrument, to free omental attachments and achieve the critical view of safety during the cholecystectomy.
The camera provides a clear view of the cystic triangle, displaying both the anterior and posterior aspects of the gallbladder.
The surgeon clears fatty tissue from the hepatic triangle, aiming to visually identify the cystic artery and cystic duct.
The surgeon begins to identify the cystic artery and cystic duct within the cystic triangle, using robotic instruments to carefully delineate these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues dissection in the cystic triangle, further clearing tissue around the cystic artery and duct before proceeding with clipping.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to free fibro fatty tissue from the hepatocystic triangle, aiming to achieve the critical view of safety by identifying two structures entering the gallbladder with the liver visible in the background.
The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum on the gallbladder, facilitating medial and lateral retraction to achieve the critical view of safety, with the cystic triangle clearly visible.
The surgeon continues to dissect the cystic duct, using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to visually differentiate the common bile duct from the cystic duct.

The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety, with the liver widely visible in the background and two distinct structures identified as the cystic artery and cystic duct entering the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz a small vein, achieving the critical view of safety with two structures entering the gallbladder visible against the liver background.

The surgeon has dissected the fibro fatty tissue from the hepatocystic triangle, revealing two distinct structures entering the gallbladder with the liver visible in the background, achieving the critical view of safety.
The surgeon prepares to place clips on the dissected cystic duct, with the critical view of safety established and the gallbladder retracted.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to confirm the anatomical structures before applying clips to the cystic duct, ensuring accurate identification of the common bile duct and cystic duct.
The surgeon applies the first clip on the cystic artery, followed by a clip on the cystic duct, with the liver and two structures clearly visible in the background.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to double clip the cystic artery and cystic duct, ensuring secure ligation of these structures with the liver visible in the background.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to divide the cystic artery and cystic duct, with the fibro fatty tissue dissected and the liver visible in the background.
The surgeon uses the coagulation function on the cystic artery and the cutting function on the cystic duct, ensuring precise division of these structures.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to retract and maneuver the gallbladder, detaching it from the liver with precision and efficiency.
The assistant removes the hook electrocautery and introduces a specimen retrieval bag into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses both robotic arms to place the gallbladder into a retrieval bag and removes it through port number three.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy procedure, having successfully removed the gallbladder from the liver bed and placed it into a retrieval bag.
